text: The surgeon uses a three-port technique for robot-assisted TAM surgery on a ventral hernia. The camera port provides a view of the surgical field, while two instrument ports facilitate manipulation. Visual cues and surface anatomy markings help identify the lateral border of the rectus abdominis. The posterior erector sheath and peritoneum complex are divided along this border.

The surgeon encounters areolar tissue while dissecting in the retromuscular plane, preserving vessels encountered along the way. The dissection progresses medially towards the junction where the posterior rectus sheath meets the anterior rectus sheath, forming the linea alba. The surgeon carefully divides the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum complex to enter the pre-peritoneal plane below the midline.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the pre-peritoneal plane, using short energy bursts to maintain the correct plane. The presence of fat confirms the correct dissection plane, while avoiding incision into the linea alba to prevent exposure of subcutaneous fat.

The surgeon continues to navigate the preperitoneal plane, using the presence of fat as a visual cue to confirm correct positioning. The camera provides a peritoneal view, revealing a sizeable amount of preperitoneal fat due to a three-centimeter defect. The plan is to place a thirteen-centimeter mesh with curved edges to address the defect.

The surgeon applies gentle traction to reduce the preperitoneal fat, clearing the defect of this tissue. A sizeable amount of preperitoneal fat is visible, often mistaken for omentum on ultrasound. The surgeon ensures complete reduction of the fat and addresses any multiple pockets, clearly defining the defect across the midline.

The surgeon continues to divide the posterior rectus sheath (PRS) in short segments, ensuring to remain in the correct anatomical plane. The dissection progresses from the left-sided retro muscular plane to the midline, entering the preperitoneal or extraperitoneal space. Inferiorly, the umbilical defect is visible, indicating the presence of a supraumbilical primary hernia.

The surgeon divides the attachment of the right-sided posterior rectus sheath (PRS) from the linea alba, creating the right-sided retro muscular plane. This marks the third plane of dissection, following the initial left-sided retro muscular plane and the midline preperitoneal or extraperitoneal space.

The surgeon further divides the right-sided posterior rectus sheath (PRS), extending the dissection to cover a small dimple at the umbilicus with mesh. This additional dissection was not initially required for the hernia repair but was performed to ensure comprehensive coverage.

The surgeon uses a six-inch scale to measure the newly created space, noting a vertical measurement of approximately fifteen centimeters. The defect is measured at two centimeters vertically and five centimeters horizontally. A V-Lock 2-0 barbed suture, made of delayed absorbable PDS, is used to close the defect in a continuous fashion, ensuring proper tissue opposition without strangulation. A small segment of the sac is included in the closure to prevent post-operative seromas. An additional suture is placed in an area perceived as loose.

A polypropylene medium weight mesh is placed for reinforcement, with pre-placed trans fascial sutures at six o'clock and twelve o'clock positions, as well as additional sutures on the sides. The surgeon uses loose, non-strangulating sutures to ensure proper mesh orientation and fixation without trapping muscle or nerve tissue.

The surgeon proceeds with the closure of the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath (PRS) complex using a continuous suturing technique with a 3-0 V-lock barbed suture made of PDS. The sutures are initially placed loosely, resembling a parachuting technique, and then tightened in one motion to create a flat suture line, ensuring a snug closure to prevent post-operative internal hernias.

The surgeon addresses laxity at the inferior end by adjusting tissue tension, taking more tissue on one side and less on the other to ensure a snug fit. The final stitches are placed to secure the closure, ensuring all peritoneal dents are closed to prevent internal herniations. The robotic platform aids in precise suturing.
